title: The Lyf of the Noble and Crysten Prynce, Charles the Grete
summary: The repository artifact is the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME CharlesG XML, corresponding to University of Michigan CME item CharlesG. U-M keyed the text in 1999 from the 1967 Oxford/EETS one-volume reprint of Sidney J. H. Herrtage's 1880-1881 two-part edition. Herrtage transcribed the complete British Library copy C.10.b.9 of William Caxton's Westminster print dated 1 December 1485 and compared it with British Library C.6.b.12, the 1483 Geneva French print. Caxton's Middle English work translates and adapts an unidentified physical copy of Jean Bagnyon's three-book French compilation; the editor's 1483 comparison copy, the 1478 first print, and the two surviving French manuscripts are kept distinct from that unidentified exemplar. Complete public IIIF bundles are cached for the 1478 BnF print and Fondation Martin Bodmer Cod. Bodmer 15, while current and legacy routes for the Caxton print, BGE Ms. fr. 188, commercial reproductions, and requested imaging are recorded without implying public image availability.
review_notes: The cataloged intellectual work is Caxton's 1485 Middle English three-book translation and adaptation, not Fierabras alone, Jean Bagnyon's French source work, Herrtage's edition, U-M's encoding, or the repository XML.

The 1880-1881 Herrtage carrier includes introduction, edited text, endnotes, and glossary. U-M's 1967 source copy encodes the title matter, introduction, and edited text through printed page 252; endnotes from pages 253-263 are integrated, and their printed pages plus the glossary on pages 265-268 are absent.

ISTC establishes one complete surviving Caxton copy and two surviving fragments. Historical descriptions calling C.10.b.9 unique are therefore interpreted as unique complete copy, not unique surviving material.

The legacy British Library complete viewer and IIIF manifest are retained as historical exact routes, but both access.bl.uk and api.bl.uk failed DNS resolution on 2026-07-14; no image payload was available to cache.

The full BnF bundle retains all 245 canvases, including covers and end matter; the textual work is mapped to canvases 9-237 and canvas 238 is the blank verso of the final printed leaf. The full Bodmer bundle retains all 261 canvases, with the work mapped to canvases 9-242 (fols. 1r-117v).

Provider image and reproduction terms remain separate from the public-domain status of the fifteenth-century texts. Local caching records research custody and does not itself authorize redistribution.

entities.label: British Library C.10.b.9, complete copy of Caxton's 1 December 1485 print

bibliographic.citation: William Caxton, The Hystory and Lyf of the Noble and Chrysten Prynce Charles the Grete (Westminster: William Caxton, 1 December 1485), British Library C.10.b.9, ISTC ic00204800.
bibliographic.title: The Hystory and Lyf of the Noble and Chrysten Prynce Charles the Grete

holding.notes: The complete copy; ISTC also records a British Library fragment (Ames I 59, leaf a2) and one leaf at the Royal Library, Windsor.

physical_description.extent: 96 leaves; a-m8; first and last leaves blank
notes: Two columns, 39 lines, type 4*:100G; the text ends on leaf 95 and leaf 96 is blank.
This is the unique complete copy, not the only surviving material.

entities.label: Unidentified physical French exemplar translated by William Caxton
entities.description: The physical printed copy of Jean Bagnyon's three-book French compilation from which Caxton made his 1485 Middle English translation. Current scholarship leaves the exact edition unresolved among five early printed candidates available to him.
notes: Missing means not presently identified, not proven destroyed.
The five candidates are Geneva prints of 1478, c. 1479, and 1483 and two Lyon printings dated 1484-1487.
It must not be merged with Herrtage's 1483 comparison copy, the 1478 first print, Cod. Bodmer 15, or BGE Ms. fr. 188 without provenance evidence.

entities.label: Jean Bagnyon's three-book French prose compilation conventionally called Histoire de Charlemagne
entities.description: An abstract work-level node for Jean Bagnyon's French prose compilation, untitled in its manuscripts, composed c. 1465-1470 for Henri Bolomier and conventionally cataloged as Histoire de Charlemagne. It connects distinct manuscripts, prints, and Caxton's unidentified physical exemplar without asserting that any surviving carrier was his copy.
notes: Histoire de Charlemagne is a conventional modern title; the manuscripts are untitled.
The long title La conqueste du grant roy Charlemaigne is first attested in later print tradition and is not treated as an authorial original title.

entities.label: British Library C.6.b.12, 1483 Geneva French print compared by Herrtage

bibliographic.citation: Jean Bagnyon, L'Histoire de Charlemagne (Geneva: Louis Cruse, 13 March 1483), British Library C.6.b.12, ISTC if00167800.

holding.notes: The sole holding listed by ISTC and the exact French print Herrtage says he used for comparison.

notes: Preface and table a2r-a5v; text a6r-o5v; colophon on o5v.
An editorial comparison witness, not identified as Caxton's physical exemplar.

entities.label: BnF Rés. Y2-76, 1478 first printed edition of Bagnyon's compilation

bibliographic.citation: Jean Bagnyon, L'Histoire de Charlemagne (Geneva: Adam Steinschaber, 28 November 1478), BnF Rés. Y2-76, ISTC if00167700.

notes: Collateral evidence for the French print tradition; not identified as Caxton's copy.

entities.label: Cologny, Fondation Martin Bodmer, Cod. Bodmer 15

date_statements.label: manuscript production
date_statements.value: last third of the fifteenth century

notes: French; the Bagnyon work occupies fols. 1r-117v.
One of two known manuscript witnesses. It is a collateral witness, not Caxton's proven exemplar.

entities.label: Geneva, Bibliothèque de Genève, Ms. fr. 188

physical_description.extent: IV + guards + 136 folios; work on fols. 1r-135v; fol. 136 blank
notes: Bâtarde script with rubrics; wooden boards covered in calf.
One of two known manuscript witnesses and documented in the BGE by the 1620 catalog. It is not Caxton's proven exemplar.

rights.basis: The current exact U-M item page says the materials are in the public domain and applies the DPLA No Copyright-United States statement.
notes: No Copyright-United States is jurisdiction-specific, not a worldwide dedication.
The embedded 1999 availability statement is recorded separately because it conflicts with the current presentation.

rights.basis: The XML header permits copies for personal, research, and teaching use and links but prohibits redistribution by commercial or nonprofit content providers without permission.
notes: This is a historical access statement, not itself proof of copyright ownership.
Its current force and relationship to U-M's current public-domain statement remain unresolved.

rights.basis: The pinned CME repository root LICENSE contains the Unlicense but describes free and unencumbered software; it does not expressly state whether the grant covers source texts, data, or XML markup.
notes: Do not broaden a software-focused repository license to historical text, U-M encoding, or provider images without clarification.

rights.basis: The text was first published in 1485; the archived British Library viewer also labeled the digitized item Public Domain and linked the Public Domain Mark.
notes: The 2023 archived viewer assertion is historical because the current endpoint is unavailable.
Current commissioned British Library images may carry separate supply terms.

rights.component: Herrtage's 1880-1881 edition, introduction, notes, and glossary
rights.basis: The complete edition was published in 1880-1881 and is delivered openly by Internet Archive.
notes: Internet Archive delivery does not establish a separate license for every scan derivative.

rights.component: 1967 issue-specific cover, typography, and any new matter
rights.basis: The 1967 issue remains commercially listed, and no open license or complete issue-specific copyright analysis was found.
notes: This does not alter the separate public-domain status of the inherited 1880-1881 text and apparatus.

rights.basis: Access is subscription-controlled by ProQuest; no open reuse license was found on the exact record.

rights.component: Gallica digital images and metadata of BnF Rés. Y2-76
rights.basis: Gallica's manifest links its reuse conditions: noncommercial reuse is free with source attribution, while commercial reuse requires a paid BnF license.
notes: The underlying 1478 French text is separately public domain by age.
The provider's reuse conditions are not converted into an open license.

rights.component: e-codices digital images of Cod. Bodmer 15
rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest declares Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 for the digital images.
notes: Fondation publication and reproduction procedures may impose additional conditions or fees; they remain distinct from the manifest license.
The fifteenth-century French text itself is separately public domain by age.

rights.component: Reproductions supplied by Bibliothèque de Genève
rights.basis: BGE rules restrict requested reproductions to private use and require written authorization for transfer, publication, or commercial use.
notes: The fifteenth-century French text is separately public domain by age.

rights.basis: The exact ARCA record states that noncommercial dissemination and reuse are free.
notes: This statement applies to the IRHT microfilm record and does not override BGE terms for newly supplied reproductions.

editorial_practices.representation: Front matter and text through page 252; integrated endnotes; printed notes and glossary omitted
editorial_practices.description: U-M includes front matter as far as possible, encodes the edited text through printed page 252, integrates endnotes from pages 253-263, and omits the printed note pages and glossary on pages 265-268.
editorial_practices.scope: Entire digital encoding relative to the 1967 source carrier

editorial_practices.source_feature: Words divided across printed pages or milestone boundaries
editorial_practices.representation: Divided words are rejoined in the encoding
editorial_practices.description: The encoding declaration states that words divided at page breaks or by milestones were joined.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Missing or inconsistent printed section milestones
editorial_practices.description: The encoding declaration says missing milestones were supplied; supplied Book I and Part i headings are also identified as U-M additions.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Herrtage's editorial supplies and comparison notes
editorial_practices.representation: Supplied material and integrated notes retained
editorial_practices.description: Herrtage's supplied text is retained, and the edition's endnotes are moved into note elements at their referenced locations.
editorial_practices.scope: Edited Middle English text and apparatus

evidence.citation: CharlesG XML, Caxton introduction, chapter table, three books, source-author envoy, and Caxton colophon.
evidence.locator: TEXT/BODY
evidence.summary: The text names Caxton as translator, William Daubeny as requester, gives completion on 18 June 1485 and printing on 1 December 1485, explains Bagnyon's three-book source design, and supplies evidence for work structure and source relationships.

evidence.citation: CharlesG XML, editorial declaration and integrated apparatus.
evidence.locator: HEADER/ENCODINGDESC/EDITORIALDECL; NOTE and NOTE1 elements
evidence.summary: Records selection, rejoined divided words, supplied milestones and headings, retained Herrtage supplies, and integration of endnotes whose printed pages are excluded.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of CharlesG repository XML.
evidence.locator: TEXT/BODY hierarchy and element counts
evidence.summary: The BODY has six DIV1, eight DIV2, and 87 DIV3 elements, representing Herrtage's introduction, Caxton's introduction, table, Books I-III, 86 numbered chapters, and an unnumbered final Caxton closer; it also has 262 PB, 376 MILESTONE, 337 NOTE1, and 10 L elements.

evidence.summary: States that Caxton used a French printed exemplar but that the exact edition is uncertain; following Lotte Hellinga, any of five early printings could have been used.

evidence.citation: Visual and structural inspection of the complete cached Cod. Bodmer 15 IIIF bundle.
evidence.locator: all 261 canvases; work canvases 9-242, fols. 1r-117v
evidence.summary: Full-color images are legible and show brown-black text, red rubrication, and initials. The complete bound object includes covers, flyleaves, binding views, edges, rulers, and color/quality-control targets.

evidence.citation: Fondation Martin Bodmer, Research and Reproductions.
evidence.locator: codex access, reproduction requests, rates, attribution, and publication
evidence.summary: Explains exceptional physical access, digital reproduction requests, treatment of already digitized documents, attribution requirements, and scholarly versus commercial publication charges.

evidence.citation: Bibliothèque de Genève, Catalogue des manuscrits français 1-198 (2011), Ms. fr. 188.
evidence.locator: printed pp. 341-343
evidence.summary: Identifies Bagnyon, dates the paper manuscript 1470-1478, describes 136 folios and physical features, maps its three parts, documents library presence by 1620, and notes the two-manuscript tradition and BGE's 1478 incunable.

evidence.citation: Biblissima record for Bibliothèque de Genève, Ms. fr. 188.
evidence.locator: manuscript metadata and digitization links
evidence.summary: Aggregates the exact manuscript record but exposes no digitized object, supporting a no-public-viewer distinction from the catalog metadata.

evidence.citation: ARCA, BGE Ms. fr. 188, IRHT microfilm MF 02146.
evidence.locator: reproduction description, coverage, frames, and rights
evidence.summary: Describes a complete 1953 black-and-white 18-frame microfilm from upper-board verso through fols. 1-136 and lower-board verso, states free noncommercial dissemination and reuse, but exposes no public image viewer.

evidence.citation: Bibliothèque de Genève, regulations for consultation and reproduction of manuscripts and rare works.
evidence.locator: registration, Salle Senebier requests, reproduction process, and permitted uses
evidence.summary: Sets free 18-plus registration with ID, request routes, reproduction fees and timing, private-use limits, and written-permission requirements for transfer, publication, or commercial use.

evidence.citation: Bibliothèque de Genève digitization guide for users outside Geneva and current imaging tariff.
evidence.locator: City portal request workflow, contacts, services, and tariffs
evidence.summary: Provides the current remote digitization request procedure, contact details, and fee schedule.

open_questions.question: Which of the five candidate early French printings did Caxton translate, and does his physical copy survive?
next_steps: Consult Keller's 1992 critical edition, Hellinga's source analysis, and later stemmatic/provenance scholarship.
Do not equate textual closeness or Herrtage's 1483 comparison with physical identity.

open_questions.question: Will the British Library restore the legacy complete Charles the Grete viewer/IIIF service or provide a current bulk digital surrogate?
next_steps: Ask British Library Digitisation Services about ark:/81055/vdc_100111693019.0x000001 and shelfmark C.10.b.9.
Request a quote and reuse terms if a current complete copy is available.

open_questions.question: How should the current U-M Public Domain / No Copyright-United States statement, the embedded 1999 restrictive availability statement, and the CME root Unlicense be reconciled for XML redistribution?
next_steps: Request scope clarification from U-M and Kyvero Press.
Keep copyright, provider access terms, and repository license assertions separate until clarified.

open_questions.question: What issue-specific changes or new matter distinguish the 1967 one-volume reprint from the 1880-1881 edition beyond binding and title matter?
next_steps: Collate a complete physical or digital 1967 copy against the cached 1880-1881 scan.
Do not describe the reprint as photographic or reset without issue-level evidence.

open_questions.question: Can a complete public digital surrogate of BGE Ms. fr. 188 be obtained or exposed through BGE/ARCA?
next_steps: Contact BGE digitization services and IRHT/ARCA citing Ms. fr. 188 and MF 02146.
Record any supplied images and their potentially differing BGE and IRHT terms as separate access layers.
